title: Coil-built garden lantern in air-dry clay
subtitle: A coiled cylinder with pierced windows, built to hold a tea-light
excerpt: A garden lantern built from coils of air-dry clay around a cylindrical former. Geometric windows are pierced at the leather-hard stage; the finished piece holds a tea-light inside and casts shadow patterns on nearby surfaces. No kiln is required.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Coil-building methods documented across ceramic traditions worldwide; pierced lantern forms appear in historical Japanese, Moroccan, and European studio pottery.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled to an even diameter by hand, used as the building unit in coil construction
The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to be carved and pierced without crumbling
Scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying a thick clay-and-water paste before joining
Fully dried unfired clay; fragile until sealed

content.text: A lantern is one of the most rewarding coil projects: the coiled cylinder is strong, the cut-out windows emerge from the solid wall at the
leather-hard

stage, and the tea-light inside picks up the window patterns on walls and table surfaces. No kiln is required. The lantern is for indoor or covered-porch use with a battery-powered tea-light, which produces no heat risk to air-dry clay.

materials.name: Cardboard tube (toilet roll or kitchen roll core) as former

content.text: Preparation

Wrap the cardboard tube in a single layer of cling film so the clay does not bond to it. Roll the tube on the board to check it sits stable. Set it upright on a small slab base cut from 6 mm clay; a 10 cm diameter circle is sufficient. Roll a batch of
coils
10 mm in diameter and 30 cm long. You will need about 12 coils for a lantern 18 cm tall.
Building the cylinder
Use
score-and-slip

to attach the first coil: scratch the base slab where the coil will sit and apply slip, then wind the first coil around the tube and press the end firmly down. Blend the bottom of the coil into the base with your finger.

Score the top of each completed coil and apply slip before adding the next. Press the new coil firmly down and blend the inside join with your fingertip in a continuous circular stroke. Use the wooden rib to smooth the outside surface every two to three coils.

Continue building to 18 cm height. Keep the cylinder vertical by checking against the tube former inside. Add a final smooth coil at the top to form the rim, slightly thicker than the wall coils for strength.

Allow the built cylinder to stiffen to leather-hard before removing the cardboard tube. To remove it, gently compress the tube inward at the top to break its contact with the clay and slide it upward.
Piercing the windows

At leather-hard, the clay is firm enough to cut cleanly. Draw your window pattern on the surface with a pencil or the needle tool; simple shapes (squares, diamonds, circles) work best. Press the needle tool through the wall and draw it around the marked shape. For circles, make a series of small cuts around the circumference, then push the disc through from inside. For squares, cut along each line and remove the tile. Smooth all cut edges with a damp sponge.
Drying and finishing
Allow the pierced lantern to dry fully to
bone-dry

: at least 2 days for a cylinder of this wall thickness. Keep it upright throughout. Apply two coats of diluted PVA inside and outside, allowing full drying between coats. Use a battery-powered tea-light only. A real flame is a fire risk in an air-dry clay vessel and produces enough heat to crack the wall.
Variations
Cut star or crescent window shapes for a Moroccan lantern feel.
Build without a cardboard former by using a rolled paper cylinder. The paper collapses inward once the clay is leather-hard, making removal easier.
Build a square lantern over a cardboard box former with slab sides instead of coils.

items.problem: Cylinder pulled inward at one side
items.cause: Coils blended unevenly, pulling the wall to one direction
items.fix: Blend the inside and outside alternately, working around the full circumference in each session

items.cause: The clay was cut before it reached leather-hard, or after it had over-dried
items.fix: Cut only at leather-hard. The clay should feel like firm cheese. If too dry, mist the area lightly and wait 10 minutes before cutting

items.problem: Lantern cracked across a window row
items.cause: Too many windows too close together, leaving a weak horizontal band
items.fix: Space windows at least 15 mm apart vertically and horizontally. Add a narrow coil reinforcement strip around the inside at the window row height
